sound_type: 'poor-will' repeated -- soft, whistled two-note phrase at 1500-2500 Hz, repeated endlessly on warm summer nights; hypnotic, metronomic; moderate amplitude audible at 200-300m; E=3
ecosystem: Dry, rocky, open terrain -- sagebrush steppe, rocky hillsides, canyon edges, dry open woodland. East side of the Cascades. Summer breeder. The only bird known to hibernate (true torpor). Cryptic camouflage. Nocturnal insect hunter.
season: Summer breeder in PNW. Hibernates in winter (unique among birds). Calling peaks warm summer nights after sunset. Nocturnal.
family: Caprimulgidae (FIRST -- NEW FAMILY. BREAKS Strigidae streak at 10. Nightjars enter the engine.)

coordinates.description: Common Poorwills breed in dry, rocky habitats east of the Cascades in Washington -- sagebrush steppe, rocky hillsides, canyon country.

taxonomy_note.description: First Common Poorwill entry. FIRST CAPRIMULGIDAE -- new family enters. BREAKS Strigidae streak at 10. First genus: Phalaenoptilus. The only bird known to hibernate. Torpor discovery by Jaeger (1946-1948). Hopi knowledge predates Western science.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Phalaenoptilus from Greek 'phalaena' (moth) + 'ptilon' (feather/wing) -- moth-winged, referring to the silent, moth-like flight. nuttallii for Thomas Nuttall, English-American naturalist.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Phalaenoptilus -- first entry. Monotypic genus in North America.
taxonomy_note.family_position: FIRST CAPRIMULGIDAE. BREAKS Strigidae streak at 10. Nightjars enter.

song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Primary call: 1500-2500 Hz. Moderate pitch. Pleasant to human ear.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual phrase: 0.5-0.7 seconds. Repeated at 1-2 second intervals. Calling bouts: 10-30+ minutes continuous.
song_metrics.note_structure: TWO-NOTE PHRASE: 'poor-will' -- first syllable lower, second rises slightly. Some hear faint third syllable ('poor-will-ip'). No variation. Endless repetition.
song_metrics.note_types: Primary call (poor-will), growl (alarm, rare), purring trill (courtship, rare).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Two-note phrase repeated without variation. 1-2 second intervals. The repetition IS the structure. Hypnotic, metronomic.
song_metrics.timbre: Soft, whistled, clear. Musical without complexity. Like a gentle whistle in the darkness.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Moderate. Clearly audible at 200-300m in still conditions. Carries well in dry, open habitat. Not loud but persistent.
song_metrics.tempo: ~0.5-1 phrases per second (1 phrase every 1-2 seconds). Metronomic regularity.
song_metrics.context: Territorial advertisement, pair communication. Males call from ground or low perch on warm summer nights. Dusk through night.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. The 'poor-will' call is consistent across the species' range.
song_metrics.key_property: HYPNOTIC REPETITION -- 'poor-will' endlessly repeated at 1500-2500 Hz. E=3. FIRST CAPRIMULGIDAE. BREAKS Strigidae streak at 10. Only hibernating bird. Cryptic camouflage. Ground-nesting. Nocturnal insectivore. East of Cascades.

plumage.overall: Gray-brown with complex pattern of buff, brown, gray, and black. Cryptic -- precisely mimics rocky, sandy ground. Short rounded tail with buffy-white corners. Large head, tiny bill, enormous gape.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Cryptic ground camouflage. Small size (smallest North American nightjar). Short rounded tail. At night: orange-red eye-shine. On ground: virtually invisible.
plumage.flight_pattern: Moth-like, buoyant, low. Short sallies from ground or low perch to capture insects. Returns to perch. Not sustained aerial flight like nighthawk.

physical_description.overall: Length: 19-21 cm. Weight: 36-58 g. Wingspan: 30-33 cm. Smallest North American nightjar. Compact, large-headed, short-tailed.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Smallest nightjar. Cryptic plumage. Short rounded tail. Orange-red eye-shine at night. Ground-sitting habit.
physical_description.field_id: Listen for 'poor-will' repeated on warm summer nights in dry, rocky habitat east of the Cascades. Look for eye-shine on roads at night. During day: virtually invisible on rocky ground.

nodes.title: BREAKS Strigidae Streak -- Caprimulgidae Enters
nodes.description: Strigidae dectet (135-144) broken at degree 145. New family: Caprimulgidae (nightjars). Owls replaced by nightjars. Vertebrate predators replaced by aerial insectivores. First Caprimulgidae in the engine.

nodes.title: Only Hibernating Bird -- True Torpor
nodes.description: The only bird known to hibernate. Body temp drops from 40C to 5C. Heart rate from ~100 to <10 bpm. Metabolic rate to 1/30th normal. Bouts approaching 100 days. Discovered by Jaeger (1946-1948). Hopi knew as 'Holchko' (the sleeping one).

nodes.title: Cryptic Camouflage -- Ground Perfection
nodes.description: Plumage precisely mimics rocky ground. No nest -- eggs on bare substrate. Won't flush until stepped on. Detected by eye-shine at night. Road-sitting for warmth. A feathered stone.

nodes.title: Caprimulgidae -- The Nightjar Family
nodes.description: Nocturnal/crepuscular insectivores. Large gape with rictal bristles. Cryptic plumage. Ground-nesters. Named for their calls. 'Caprimulgus' = goat-milker (myth). Three PNW species: Poorwill, Nighthawk, Lesser Nighthawk.

cross_references.relationship: STREAK BREAK! Strigidae at 144 (10th consecutive, record). Caprimulgidae at 145 (new family). The owls end and the nightjars begin. Both nocturnal insectivores but different families.
SECOND CAPRIMULGIDAE! Poorwill (145, E=3, ground-sitter) to Nighthawk (146, E=5, aerial). Same family, opposite strategies. The nightjar pair.
FAMILY TRANSITION! GHO started the Strigidae streak at 135. Poorwill ends it at 145 by introducing Caprimulgidae. Predator to insectivore.
